data.title: Tennant Company Will Clean Up
data.description:
Back in early September, one company that I decided to take another look at was Tennant Company (TNC). This is a fascinating enterprise, at least to me because not only is it one of the oldest companies out there, having been founded back in 1870, but also because of its overall business model. The firm produces and sells manual and autonomous mechanized cleaning equipment that is used by industrial and commercial clients. It also makes other things such as detergent free cleaning technologies, aftermarket parts and consumables, and more. The firm provides certain maintenance and repair services, and it even offers up financing, rental, and leasing programs for customers. There are other things as well, but you get the idea. At the end of the day, this is a large player with over 40,000 customers in over 100 different countries.

Leading up to that point in early September, I had been bullish about the business. This was even though top-line and bottom-line results for the enterprise had been worsening. And it was based on the company's valuation, both on an absolute basis and relative to other similar firms. Unfortunately, the market has disagreed with my assessment, pushing the stock down 12.2% since that article was published. By comparison, the S&P 500 is up 5.1%. As disappointing as this is, however, I maintain my bullish stance. This is a quality company that should continue to grow in the long run. And when you add on top of that its valuation, a bullish assessment just makes sense.

Shares Will Clean Up

The only new data that we currently have regarding Tennant Company covers through the third quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue amounted to $303.3 million. That's a decline of 4% compared to the $315.8 million the company reported a year earlier. To really understand this drop, however, we need to dig deeper. The real weak spot, as the chart below illustrates, would be volume. This took an 8.2% hit during the quarter compared to the same time last year.

According to management, volume declines were prevalent in North America. Management chalked this up to what it called the lapping of a "significant backlog reduction benefit" that the company achieved the previous year. What this means, in short, is that in 2024, management worked hard to bring the backlog down, knowing that it would be essentially front-loading revenue at the expense of the future. This is not all that surprising to me. During and shortly after the COVID-19 pandemic, there was significant investment in anything hardware related. Companies across every industry that I am aware of made heavy investments because of a surge in demand as the economy reopened. It appears to me as though Tennant Company was no exception to this and was, in fact, a beneficiary of this trend.

By comparison, other regions performed much better. The closest to the Americas' region in terms of volume declines ended up being the Asia Pacific region, with a drop of 6.4% when it came to overall organic sales. Management blamed this on reduced commercial equipment volume in China and industrial equipment volume in South Korea. I know for a fact that China's economy has been slowing down. So none of that is surprising. South Korea, meanwhile, has been embroiled in the last couple of years in political crises, and it is suffering from significant population decline. So that's also not terribly shocking. By comparison, the EMEA (Europe, Middle East, and Africa) regions for the company performed much better, with sales actually rising 5.5%. But this was only because of foreign currency fluctuations. Actual organic sales dipped, but only slightly, with a decline of 0.4%, thanks to lower volumes in some of its markets. But in others, like the UK and Southern Europe, volumes improved.

The good news is that some of the pain from a volume standpoint was offset by other changes. As an example, higher prices that management was able to implement pushed revenue up by 2.8%. Some of this was because of tariffs that management decided to push on to its customers. However, the company also mentioned that the beginning of the year's price actions played a big role in this improvement also. We also saw a 1.4% benefit on top of this when it came to foreign currency fluctuations.

As revenue declined, profitability for the company also worsened. But this requires context. Actual GAAP net profits contracted from $20.8 million to $14.9 million. But if we adjust for certain one-time items, we actually get an increase from $26.6 million to $27.3 million. The company benefited from a slightly higher gross profit margin as management made sure to raise prices by more than just tariff increases. But that's not all. During the quarter, the company was able to add back higher ERP modernization costs that ended up being $2.4 million above what they were the previous year. They were also able to add back a $4 million legal contingency cost that involved a legal settlement that management had to pay out involving its intellectual property. And finally, during the most recent quarter, there was a $1 million restructuring-related expense as management continues to focus on certain strategic initiatives and long-term objectives that management has put into place.

Other profitability metrics for the company were approved. Operating cash flow did admittedly fall from $30.7 million to $28.7 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $39.4 million to $44 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business grew from $47.9 million to $49.8 million. In the chart above, you can also see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time last year.

This is where things do look a little different. Just like with what we saw for the third quarter, volume declines were problematic here. But the company did not benefit from as high a price increase on the goods that it sells. That boosted sales by only 1.5%. And foreign currency fluctuations added nothing to the top line. As a result of these items, profitability for the company worsened across the board. This is certainly discouraging, to say the least.

Management does expect some weakness to continue throughout the rest of this year. They said, for instance, that organic revenue should fall by between 3% and 5%. However, the firm does expect a very small improvement in its EBITDA margin, which would likely translate to EBITDA of $202.5 million. And if we project our results based on what we have seen already for the year, we would likely get adjusted net profits of $110.4 million and adjusted operating cash flow of $130.4 million.

Using these estimates, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. These multiples aren't fantastic, but they certainly aren't bad. Relative to other firms that have similarities to it, Tennant Company is also attractively priced. In the table below, I compared it to five such enterprises. On both a price-to-earnings basis and on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, Tennant Company ended up being the cheapest of the group. Meanwhile, on a price-to-operating cash flow basis, only one of the five businesses ended up coming in cheaper.

Company Price/Earnings Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
Tennant Company 12.1 10.3 7.3
Mueller Industries (MLI) 16.5 16.4 11.6
Mayville Engineering Company (MEC) 29.4 5.5 13.6
SPX Technologies (SPXC) 46.1 33.8 23.1
The Timken Company (TKR) 19.3 10.4 9.4
Parker-Hannifin (PH) 30.7 29.3 23.1

In the short term, we should expect some weakness. But long term, I am confident in the company's potential. Not only is the stock cheap. Also, management is working on some very interesting initiatives. For instance, the firm is actively growing its AMR robotics business, with year-to-date sales up 9% and unit volumes rising by 25%. This is thanks to certain product lines like its X4 and X6 ROVR offerings, as well as major strategic customer wins globally. Another example would be the T360 mid-sized walk-behind scrubber that the company launched during the third quarter of this year. And on top of all of this, the company continues to see the successful rollout of its ERP system, with the Asia Pacific region experiencing the first go-live for this technology. The goal here that management has is to improve decision-making, to deliver better customer experiences, to boost cybersecurity, and to prepare the company to deploy AI offerings in the future. This kind of innovation has the potential to add significant upside to shareholders if it ultimately bears fruit.

Takeaway

As things stand, I must say that I am quite optimistic about Tennant Company. Yes, it is true that recent financial performance has been mixed and perhaps even slightly disappointing. But on the whole, this is a fascinating company that is doing reasonably well in a very tough environment. Shares are cheap, and the innovations that management continues to invest in should create even more value in the long run. Added all together, and I believe that maintaining Tennant Company as a "Buy" candidate is the right move.

data.title: Delta Is Trading Like A Risky Airline While Operating Like A Quality Business
data.description:
Back in July, I called Delta (DAL) a Buy at $56. I liked the recovery story, the way the company was keeping costs down and how it was making more from premium seats and loyal customers. Since then, the stock’s jumped 20%. It’s beaten the S&P and most rivals. The numbers keep coming in strong: higher profit margins, solid cash flow and management keeps raising and reaffirming their outlook. Even with this run-up, I still think Delta’s worth buying. Here’s why: At $68, you’re paying only about 11 times 2025’s expected earnings. That’s not much for a business this steady. I think most people still don’t see just how much money Delta can make in a normal year. This isn’t the old Delta, bouncing all over with the economy. Premium seats, the loyalty program and a tighter grip on costs make this a steadier, less risky business. Some people worry that good times can’t last, that costs and wages will eat up profits. But looking at the numbers, I think those worries are overdone. If demand or margins surprise to the upside (especially for international and premium cabins) this stock can go higher. Right now, I see more to gain than lose, with a realistic shot at a 15 to 25% return over the next year or two.

Delta’s Strengths

Delta is everywhere. Its main hubs are in Atlanta, Detroit, Minneapolis, Salt Lake City and it’s strong on the coasts too. Most of the company’s money still comes from flights inside the U.S., but international travel (especially across the Atlantic and to popular vacation spots) is growing steadily. Delta’s biggest strengths are its loyalty program and credit card deals, which brought in $6 billion in cash this year. This steady money lets Delta offer better service and push premium seats, which keeps customers coming back. The company’s also gotten smart with spending and paying off debt. This year, it’s already cut net debt by over $1 billion. New lounges, upgraded planes and more premium seats have led to higher revenue and better profits. Management says their return on investment is 13% for the past year. That’s something you don’t see much in airlines.

There’s always risk in airlines, but Delta has done a better job than most. Right now, airlines aren’t just flooding the market with cheap seats. They’re being careful and cutting flights that don’t make money. That helps everyone, especially big, well-known companies like Delta. Business travel is picking up again, with Delta’s business travel revenue growing about 8% over last year. Strong demand for flights to Europe and South America is helping offset weaker spots in the U.S., especially in long-haul markets. The recent government shutdown and FAA flight caps are a headache and will cost Delta around $200 million this quarter, but the company is big enough and has enough cash to handle it. Delta is better protected than most competitors and thanks to its loyalty program and premium cabins, Delta usually makes more profit per customer. Fuel prices still matter, but owning its own refinery helps soften the blow. All in all, Delta’s position means it will likely keep earning more than rivals.

Financials

Delta’s revenue for the first nine months of 2025 is $47.4 billion, up nearly 3%. Profits are up too, with net income of $3.79 billion and earnings per share of $5.80. That’s much better than last year. Free cash flow is $2.8 billion so far and management says it could reach up to $4 billion for the year. The company has $3.8 billion in cash, another $3.1 billion it could borrow if needed and debt keeps shrinking. Net debt is now down to about $11 billion. Debt compared to profits is about 2.4 times on a gross basis. That’s a lot safer than in the past. The domestic business still brings in the most money, but the real growth is coming from premium cabins and the loyalty side (which are more profitable). Costs are under control and profits should stay strong, even with wage hikes and investments in new planes. Even after the government shutdown hit, Delta should still earn around $6 per share this year.

At $68, Delta’s price is about 11 times 2025’s expected earnings and just over 9 times what it should make in 2026. That multiple still reflects a much riskier business than Delta is today. I think the company can hit $7.25 in earnings per share in 2026 (slightly higher than what most analysts expect), thanks to premium seats and keeping costs tight. If people start to see how steady this business is, I think the stock could trade between $78 and $85 in the next year or so. That’s 15 to 25% upside, not even counting the 1% dividend. If the company buys back stock, it could go higher. The price right now just doesn’t reflect how much safer and more reliable Delta is compared to the past.

Market Mood

People are more positive about Delta now. Analysts are starting to upgrade and there’s talk about bigger dividends and strong earnings. There’s still some doubt, especially in the news, with worries about costs and regulations. The $200 million hit from the shutdown did push the stock down for a bit. But with management standing by their full-year outlook and talk of an airline “super cycle” people are warming up to the stock again. Money has been flowing in, but there’s not a ton of hype so the price could rise if Delta keeps delivering.

The big risks: a recession, oil prices spiking, or government rules cutting into flights. Flight cuts and the shutdown this year will hurt profits, but the hit is small compared to how much Delta makes overall. The company’s in a better spot to handle these shocks than most. There’s always a chance that rivals could start fare wars or that new rules or mistakes could make the stock fall. If fuel prices jump, earnings could drop by 40 to 60 cents per share. If the stock’s value drops by a point, that’s about $6 or $7 off the price. But Delta has a good track record and its strong cash flow and lower debt give it a buffer.

Outlook

Looking ahead, I expect Delta to keep benefiting from premium and business travel, more international flights and the strong loyalty program. The main things to watch are how Delta handles the current flight caps and shutdown fallout, whether it keeps paying down debt and if it can keep profit margins up. If international travel or premium cabins do even better than expected, or if the company pays off debt faster, the stock could really surprise people. If there’s a recession or fuel prices spike, it could hurt. But I think people still don’t realize how strong and steady Delta’s earnings have become.

In a bad year, say there’s a recession or higher fuel costs, earnings might drop to $5 a share and the stock could fall to about $45. In a “normal” year, I see Delta making $7.25 per share and trading at $78 to $85. If things go great, no recession, business travel bounces back and people get more excited about airlines again, earnings could hit $8 and the stock could top $90. The biggest factor isn’t Delta’s execution: it’s what happens in the world around it.

Bottom line, I still think Delta is a Buy. I don’t think the stock fully reflects how much better the company is today. Free cash flow is strong, the dividend is growing and Delta’s in a much less risky spot than before. If profit margins shrink or rules make it harder for Delta to compete, I’d rethink. Otherwise, I see a clear path to strong returns.

data.title: Blue Bird: Tougher FY2026 Comparison, Balanced By Richer Margins And Expanded Capacity
data.description:
I previously covered Blue Bird Corporation (BLBD) in August 2025, discussing their ability to mitigate the ongoing tariff war/regulatory headwinds surrounding the pulled funding for electric school buses, as observed in their robust pricing power.

Combined with the well-diversified school bus platform across diesel, gasoline, gas, propane, and EV, I had believed that the company remained well positioned to outperform ahead - resulting in my reiterated Buy rating then.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the BLBD stock, thanks to their robust execution/performance metrics despite the industry wide headwinds - all of which already contributed to the richer profit margins.

Combined with the tailwinds from the raised prices and the planned expansion in their production capacity, I am of the opinion that the company is likely to outperform as observed in the management's promising long-term (FY2029/FY2030) guidance, despite the near-term risks from the moderating backlog and the tougher YoY comparison in FY2026.

BLBD Outperforms Despite Regulatory/Tariff Headwinds

BLBD 1Y Stock Price

Since my Buy rating, the BLBD stock has indeed charted a lumpy stock price movement after hitting a new 52 week height of $61s and then moderating nearer to my prior fair-value estimates of $43.50, as posited in my last article.

This development is unsurprising indeed, attributed to the ongoing regulatory headwind facing the EV sector, with the expiry of the 45W tax credit already triggering the notably moderating EV backlog from 1,200 buses in FQ3'25 to 901 buses in FQ4'25, despite the management highlighting the intact EPA funding for round 4/5.

This reason may also be why the company has reported a moderating alternative power sales mix of 56% in FQ4'25 (-5 points QoQ/-2 YoY), with it lending credence to my prior conjecture surrounding the potential demand shift towards conventional fuel platforms.

Otherwise, I am of the opinion that BLBD remains well positioned to weather the headwinds, with the raised prices already triggering higher revenues of $152.6K per unit in FQ4'25 (+16.3% YoY/+76.6% from FY2019 levels of $86.4K).

It is unsurprising then, that the company has also reported the expanding gross margins of 21.1% (+4.1 points YoY/+8.1 from FY2019 levels of 13%) and adj EBITDA margins of 16.6% (+4.8 points YoY/+8.6 from FY2019 levels of 8%).

Combined with the higher units sold at 9,409 in FY2025 (+4.5% YoY) and the long-term (FY2029/FY2030) guidance of expanded capacity at between "12,000 to 13,500 units," I am of the opinion that the company is well positioned to outperform moving forward.

BLBD Valuations

Based on the FY2025 adj EBITDA margin performance at 15% (+1.4 points YoY/+7 from FY2019 levels of 8%) and the management's FY2026 guidance at 15% (inline YoY), I am of the opinion that BLBD is extremely discounted at current levels of FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 6.79x.

This is compared to the 5Y mean of 17.30x with an average adj EBITDA margins of ~7.6% and the 4Y pre-pandemic mean of 7.34x at ~9.3%, respectively, with the stock deserving an upgraded valuation given the richer margins.

Readers must also note that BLBD has hinted at their ability to achieve the long-term adj EBITDA margin target of 16%, with it highlighting the resilience of its pricing power/operating leverage despite the tariff risks.

Combined with the extremely cheap estimated long-term EV/EBITDA valuations of 4.9x, based on the Enterprise Value of $1.47B by the time of writing and the management's long-term adj EBITDA guidance of $300M at the midpoint, I am of the opinion that the stock remains highly compelling for deep-value oriented investors.

Risks Moving Into FY2026

Readers must note that BLBD's upcoming expansion in production capacity is expected to trigger a notable Free Cash Flow generation impact, as observed in the management's preliminary FY2026 guidance at $20M at the midpoint (-86.9% YoY) attributed to the new plant capex.

This is good news in my opinion, since the capex is likely to be funded by cash flows while allowing the company to continue reporting a stable balance sheet health against the net cash position of $138.99M observed in FY2025 (+325.1% YoY).

At the same time, BLBD's expanded production capacity comes at a time when school bus fleets are aging and needing replacements, as observed in the "average school bus age is approximately 11 years, and half the current fleet is already 10 years or older" against the US "states set formal replacement targets at 15 years and most buses have an expected lifespan of 12–15 years."

This is especially since the management has hinted at intensified monetization opportunities beyond the school bus segment to the commercial segment through Micro Bird, with things likely to be material from FY2027 onwards.

On the other hand, I urge readers to monitor BLBD's overall backlog, as observed in the notably moderating backlog at 3.9K units as of FQ4'25 earnings call on November 19, 2025, albeit up from the FY2025 levels of 3.1K (-35.4% YoY).

Readers may want to note that the backlog growth early in the FQ1'26 quarter is attributed to the management "recently locking our tariff charges for new orders with deliveries until the end of June 2026," with it potentially triggering headwinds to its bottom-line performance if tariffs/material costs rise.

Combined with the likely to be tougher YoY comparison in FY2026, pending the accretion of its expanded manufacturing capacity over the next few years, I am of the opinion that BLBD is likely to report stagnant performance metrics in the near-term.

As a result, I urge readers to temper their near-term expectations heading into FY2026.

BLBD Remains A Great Buy

BLBD 5Y Stock Price

For now, BLDB's profitable growth cadence may be why the bulls have been defending the stock's ongoing +68.2% recovery from the April 2025 bottom along with the recent consolidation at the 50/100 day moving averages of $50s.

BLBD Technical Indicators

Thanks to the recent sideways trading since late October 2025, BLBD looks a lot more compelling here, based on the reasonable RSIs and the moderating trading volumes - as the bulls seemingly defend a floor at the $50s after the -18.4% correction from the 52 weeks heights.

Based on the FY2025 adj EPS performance of $4.38 (+26.5% YoY) and the current FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 11.97x, it is apparent that the stock is still trading near to my fair value estimates of $52.40.

At the same time, based on my long-term (FY2029/FY2030) adj EPS estimates of $5.74, there appears to be an excellent upside potential of +35.9% to my long-term BLBD price target of $68.70 as well.

My calculation is based on the management's long-term (FY2029/FY2030) production guidance of 12.75K units at the midpoint, the FQ4'25 revenues of $152.6K per unit, the subsequent estimated revenues of $1.94B, the FY2025 net income margins of 9.7%, the estimated long-term net income performance of $188.70M, and the FQ4'25 shares outstanding of 32.84M.

As a result of the stable profitable growth trends no matter the regulatory/tariff headwinds, the expanding production capacity well supported by the robust backlog/aging school bus fleet, and the promising bullish support at current levels of $50s, I am reiterating my Buy rating for the BLBD stock here.

data.title: Babcock International: A Reasonable European Defense Play
data.description:
Babcock International Group PLC (BCKIF) is not a bad way to play European defense, but it's not relatively undervalued. It is more a bet on the general trend, which is that at least for the time being and assuming that the current ideological gulf between the US and Europe persists, European defense should receive a special tailwind from needing to support a previously less self-sufficient European defense complex.

Looking at European defense multiples, the premium is clearly with the more industrial players that can benefit more from expected scale. Babcock is not among this number and is therefore cheaper, but this discount is justified and not an especially great bargain. However, while BAE (BAESF) has a more industrial angle than Babcock, likely earning their slightly premium multiple, Babcock is benefiting from a secular and credible margin lift from phasing out poorly priced projects.

We like Babcock but only because it is in line with plays in European defense. However, we think European defense becomes a bigger opportunity once the Ukraine war ends, and the potentially more secular European defense picture will perhaps go underappreciated.

Brief on the Political Angle

It is unavoidable to discuss politics and geopolitics in the context of defense as it is essential for businesses like Babcock that are contracted by governments and dependent on NATO spending, but also discretionary military spending logic tied to broader geopolitical considerations. The takeaways at the moment are this:

  • The current US political institution is ideologically opposed to European institutions and the so-called European elites, and it is reflected in the US posture of greater isolationism and the greater Atlantic tensions as the European core is not capitulating in their geopolitical positions. The reactions are quite similar and reflect a continuing ideological gulf that became very evident at the Munich security conference.
  • Beyond the floating of the 5% GDP target for spending for NATO, where many European nations were derelict in meeting the lower 2% target currently established, the US is also publishing more formally, such as in its latest national security strategy, their intention for a more marginal role in global peacemaking.

The bottom line is that the reasonable market actor should expect more spending to be done on defense, and for Europe to prefer to spend on its own military-industrial complex as opposed to the US'. Therefore, European defense picks, including UK defense picks, where the UK is aligned with continental Europe in their geopolitical and political stances, with a key issue of course being the Ukraine war, are all likely to receive a sustained secular tailwind.

Babcock Results

This, of course, is already being reflected in the results of defense players in Europe that are seeing solid revenue growth and stable, if not growing, backlogs reflecting growing orders.

Briefly on Babcock, they are a complex engineering company, with around three-quarters of revenue in defense markets, quite evenly split between servicing and building ships, training aviation and servicing, production of military land vehicles like the Jackal, and dealing with the UK nuclear submarine fleet. Their defense exposure is driven by the nuclear submarine business, and there is a nice opportunity in the establishment of AUKUS which effectively guarantees the ongoing strategic interest in maintaining a nuclear submarine axis that can be supported by businesses like Babcock through missile tubes and other things like that. Their civil exposures also contain a business associated with the growing nuclear trend through Cavendish, which can also be considered a strategic market not that different in terms of secular expectations to defense. They also do other engineering within the land segment for things like power generation and mining equipment as well as engineering for rail and transport networks.

Something to note about Babcock is that other than the growth in their businesses and more demand for the services of defense contractors, and incidentally for power generation and some of their other civil activities, they are undergoing a deliberate restructuring in the way they bid for contracts because they have a backlog of contracts liquidating quite unprofitably. However, those are vanishing from the mix and the share of backlog liquidation happening from new contracts that liquidate profitably is growing; therefore, margins are experiencing some helpful growth, with more growth targeted. This is the explanation for much of their overall margin growth and profit growth, though revenues are also up 7% as of the H2. One of these poorly quoted contracts is for the Type 31, which will only emerge from 0% unit margin by next year or later when a few more units have been produced. That is a secular margin driver as well, as it's been weighing on profitability for a while, to our understanding.

Land is suffering a bit from revenue pressure from a weaker civil transport business, but otherwise there is stable demand from defense. Revenues are down 10% here due to the civil exposure and exposure to geographies like South Africa, where demand has fallen. Margins have been preserved, though, so operating profit is falling less than revenue. This is also the business where they make Jackals, which has Ukraine war implications as Jackals are being built to replaced vehicles gifted to Ukraine. On the DSG program, there is expected to be a return to growth in the H2 after a flat H1. They got a contract extension here recently which is why it's remobilizing, and to our understanding the terms should now be better for Babcock.

The smaller aviation business is experiencing significant growth as there is more French military helicopter activity, where this business delivers military pilot training and through-life support to assets for the French military in addition to the UK's. This also includes the delivery of the Mentor 2 contract that they won last year. They also have a civil exposure to Canada for ambulance helicopters where they are leasing Leonardo (FINMF) helicopters to BC and are going to provide MRO on that. Contract renegotiations and higher profitability on newer contract almost doubled this smaller segment's operating profit.

Nuclear growth is actually being driven by civil (25% growth) thanks to Cavendish getting commissions for civil nuclear projects in line with the energy transition. Beyond that support activity for subs is also increasing a lot, actually by 31%, also growing the revenues. The weakness is from infrastructure markets here due to surge activity last year with dock delivery revenues falling due to nearing completion now. Profits are picking up on improved contract mix effects. There was also a defuel order here, though backlog has fallen in this segment due to less large multi-year orders.

Marine is doing great with both stable-ish backlogs amid revenue growth and execution. Additional services for Skynet, a UK space program, are supporting backlogs, as well as support activity for the Australian navy. But large, long-term contracts are down as last year was a surge in shipbuilding activity, but they had additional demand for contracted marine services, and delivery has been stable in supporting fleets like that of the Type 31, which is actually one of the programs they are still liquidating at 0% margin and has been a headwind for years. The first ship has been delivered, and the second ship is expected for within this FY. The third ship is where a better margin might start appearing and production might be more smooth in line with the original expectations of margin for the program. But it will be a headwind for the remainder of the year as well in terms of margin. Engineering in civil markets for liquid gas equipment has been doing well, though, on the back of a surge of contract wins last year. Those have around 12-18 months to be fully delivered, but there are more wins.

Bottom Line

The company has updated its operating margin guidance to 9% up from the medium-term target of 8% since they have almost already arrived there.

Even when including pension liabilities and leases they are not a heavily indebted business.

We like Babcock and the business is altogether quite recurring since the existing base of assets needs ongoing support, and as said we do believe defense is a secularly well positioned market. In terms of valuation, the more industrial picks like Saab (SAABF) and most of all Rheinmetall (RNMBF) have considerably higher multiples at 36x and 38x EV/EBIT respectively. Babcock and BAE are around 16x and 17x respectively. But this discount is justified because profits have more room to inflect on a greater scale, which the markets expect. However, we do like the extra benefit that Babcock gets from better contract mix incoming as legacy contracts are phased out. Moreover, some of the businesses are indeed quite industrial, such as vehicle production, and the profile of support revenues is quite good. Moreover, the civil markets are actually where growth is good, and the defense markets, which are lagging in growth, will not disappoint in the medium-to-long term.

However, we are not buying Babcock. Other than the lack of a sharp relative angle, we think that there may be better opportunities to buy European defense later when the Ukraine war hopefully ends, which should bring some selling pressure for European defense (although conditions of the end of the war may mean a different reaction takes place), and it also gives us time to see how the ideological gulf between the US and Europe will develop, which we believe is going to be a driver for preferential spending by European governments on European defense as opposed to US defense, which in many areas is more advanced. We also prefer a little bit the business profile of BAE to Babcock, which has some more software exposure and, we feel, is a little more industrially oriented with exposure to MBDA, which is world leading in missiles, and to munitions manufacture, with plenty of recurring support activities as well.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Li Auto: Deep-Value Buy Supported By Robust Backlog And Expanding Capacity
data.description:
I previously covered Li Auto (LI) in October 2025, discussing how the underwhelming FQ3'25 guidance and the declining August 2025 deliveries had triggered the stock's recent underperformance compared to its pure-play EV peers.

Despite this, I had reiterated my Buy rating upon a moderate retracement, attributed to their strategic entry into the EV segment, the profitable growth trends, the rich cash flows, and the healthy balance sheet.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the LI stock here, with the oversold technical indicators and the established 5Y trading floor of $17s potentially offering outsized capital appreciation prospects, aided by the overly discounted valuations.

Combined with the expanding production capacity and the improving supply chains entering FY2026, I am of the opinion that the automaker is likely to report robust sales performance as they monetize their EV backlog, despite the likely to be lumpy bottom-line performance, the policy risks, and the changing consumer demand.

LI's Future Recovery Likely to Be Lumpy

LI 1Y Stock Price

Since my last Buy rating upon a moderate retracement to my Buy Zones of $21 ranges, the bears have proven my bullish thesis wrong indeed.

This is with the LI stock breaching those intermediate support levels and retesting the 5Y bottom of $17s, aside from the one-time breach to the $13s floor in October 2022.

Part of the pessimism may be warranted, as consumer demand "shifts more decisively toward fully electric platforms" with it negating LI's prior hybrid vehicle investment thesis worsened by the slower ramp-up of its EV offerings.

The same has been embedded in its impacted delivery numbers at 33.18K in November 2025 (+4.4% MoM/-31.9% YoY) at a time when its direct peers are reporting robust double-digit delivery growth, including XPeng (XPEV), NIO (NIO), and Xiaomi (XIACY) (XIACF), aside from BYD Company (BYDDF).

LI has further fed the pessimism with an impacted production leverage, with the lower sold volumes at stable estimated ASPs of ~277.86K Yuan (+6.7% QoQ/-1% YoY) already triggering "the higher per unit manufacturing cost from lower production volume" and the mixed adj vehicle margins of 19.8% (-1.1 points QoQ/+0.4 YoY), after omitting the Li Mega recall impact.

If anything, the recall has also triggered the automaker's impacted GAAP vehicle margins to 15.5% (-3.9 points QoQ/-5.4 YoY), GAAP operating margins to -4.3% (-7 points QoQ/-12.3 YoY), and net loss per ADS at 0.62 Yuan (-160.1% QoQ/-123.3% YoY).

As a result of the near-term noise surrounding the "supply chain bottlenecks and costs related to the recall," it is unsurprising that the LI stock has experienced a deeper pullback than expected.

At the same time, readers must note the ongoing supply glut/pricing war observed in the domestic EV sector, which has previously contributed to the suspended "trade-in subsidies for car buyers" in June 2025, worsened by the change in the government's future policies:

Beijing has outlined a new consumption plan emphasizing "intelligent and connected" NEVs but has not committed to fresh subsidy rounds, adding a layer of uncertainty for automakers already contending with intensifying competition, rapid model turnover, and tighter scrutiny of safety and supplier practices.

After January 1, 2026, new energy vehicles (battery EVs, plug-in hybrids, and fuel-cell vehicles) in China lose their full exemption from the government's vehicle purchase tax and move to a 50% exemption, with the per-vehicle benefit capped at about RMB15,000 through 2027, from a prior level of RMB30,000. Further phasing out of incentives from Beijing could also be on the way. (Seeking Alpha)

I am of the opinion that H1'26 may potentially bring forth a tougher YoY comparison for LI, with the reduction in tax exemption likely to trigger impacted consumer demand and, consequently, lower delivery volumes.

If anything, a similar trend has been observed in the US, with the expiry of the $7.5K tax credit by the end of September 2025 already triggering a notably lower US light vehicle sales by -6.5% MoM/-4.5% YoY in October 2025 - with it hinting at a painful normalization period in the near term:

Many automakers have relied on the incentives to boost consumer demand for EVs, which they've spent billions of dollars developing even as the vehicles remain largely unprofitable.

Industry analysts and executives have said they believe EV sales can continue to grow in the future, but that there will soon be a boom-and-bust situation regarding demand for electric vehicles before there's a new normal. (CNBC)

Pending the materialization of the natural hybrid/EV demand in China as subsidies taper and LI's ability to actively compete in the intensifying EV space through expanded offerings beyond the current Li Mega, Li i8, and Li i6, I am of the opinion that the stock's near-term recovery prospects appear to be mixed.

This is especially since LI faces an uncertain future based on Li Mega's safety concerns, arising from the recent fire risks and the aggressive recall of approximately half of its units sold.

These have triggered notable reputational headwinds, consequently impacting Li Mega delivery trends by -64.2% MoM/-21.2% YoY in November 2025 - partly attributed to the ongoing recalls/battery replacements and the likely lumpy bottom-line performance in the near term.

Combined with the softer demand for the Li i8, with the management having to cut prices "following disappointing initial reception" and the i8's notably lower monthly delivery target of 6K vehicles, it is unsurprising that the stock has underperformed thus far.

Elevated Orders & FQ4'25 Guidance Imply Robust Demand

On the other hand, while LI has suffered from the slower ramp-up in its battery supply/EV production trends, with it delaying their delivery and monetization cadence despite the outsized consumer demand for Li i6, I am of the opinion that the headwinds are temporary.

This is because the management has highlighted their Li i6 production capacity growth to 20K units from early 2026 onwards, allowing them to service the 70K accumulated orders moving forward.

At the same time, readers may want to note that LI has hinted at sequentially improved December 2025 delivery numbers of 40.06K at the midpoint (+20.7% MoM/-31.5% YoY), based on the FQ4'25 delivery guidance of 105K vehicles at the midpoint (+12.6% QoQ/-33.8% YoY) and the October/November 2025 cumulative deliveries at 64.94K.

This number marks a notable improvement in its delivery numbers from the trough FQ3'25 performance at 93.21K as well (-16% QoQ/-39% YoY).

The Consensus Forward Estimates

The same has been observed in the promising consensus forward estimates, with LI expected to report robust top-line growth at a CAGR of +10.4% through FY2027 as they monetize their new EV models and the elevated backlogs.

Even so, the automaker is likely to report lumpy bottom-line performance as similarly reflected above, attributed to:

  • the ongoing Li Mega recall,
  • the company covering the "purchase tax difference for i6 customers who locked in their orders in 2025 but take deliveries in 2026," and
  • the potentially softer customer demand in H1'26 (arising from the pulled-forward orders before the expiry of the government subsidies at the end of 2025) and the temporarily impacted manufacturing leverage.

As a result of its likely mixed performance metrics, I urge readers to temper their near-term expectations, since LI's reversal is likely to be prolonged.

The silver lining to its recovery thesis will be the rich balance sheet with a net cash position of $12.53B (-9.9% YoY), with it implying its ability to weather the near-term demand/delivery headwinds.

LI Offers a Deep-Value Buy Thesis

LI 5Y Stock Price

For now, LI's 5Y chart appears extremely underwhelming indeed, as the stock lost all of its gains while trading below the 50/100/200-day moving averages and retesting the $17s support levels by the time of writing.

LI Valuations

Otherwise, thanks to the -47.4% meltdown from the 52-week heights and the likely robust top-line performance, it is undeniable that LI already looks extremely compelling at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 0.39x, compared to the 5Y mean of 3.08x and the sector median of 1.34x.

When comparing LI at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 0.39x and the consensus 3Y sales growth estimates at a CAGR of +10.4% through FY2027 against its direct automaker peers, including:

  • BYDDF at 0.94x/+13.8%,
  • NIO at 1.15x/+30.6%,
  • XPEV at 1.45x/+48.7%, and
  • Xiaomi at 1.89x/+23.8%, respectively,

it goes without saying that the former has been overly discounted at current levels, with it offering an excellent deep-value buy opportunity.

A similar trend may also be observed in LI's estimated FY2027 EV/Sales valuations of 0.22x, based on the current Enterprise Value of $6.28B and the consensus FY2027 revenue estimates of $27.51B, with it highlighting its potentially outsized capital appreciation prospects upon the normalization in its delivery trends and the reversal in market sentiments.

Combined with the improved margin of safety arising from the oversold technical indicators across the RSIs and the trading volumes, I am reiterating my Buy rating for the LI stock here.

data.title: Vail Resorts: A High-Risk Yield Story Entering A Strategic Reset
data.description:
Thesis

Vail Resorts, Inc. (MTN) is in a period of restructuring. The model combines aggressive changes in marketing strategy, more dynamic pricing policies and significant investments in technology and operational efficiency.

The latest quarter has shown a set of mixed signals.

On one hand, the company showed improved pass sales participation in terms of revenue and increased traffic in Australia. It also maintained its EBITDA estimates for 2026.

On the other hand profitability was under pressure, costs have increased and weather conditions in the U.S. have created unusually high uncertainty.

In this context, Vail Resorts presents itself as a company moving towards a renewed business model but still seeking stability in execution and demand.

The stock is already generously valued and the investment narrative remains strong but with substantial risks.

Below i will analyze in details my Hold recommendation.

Financials

The financial performance in fiscal Q1 reflects the changes in the operating environment and the new strategy.

Revenues increased by approximately 4% YOY, indicating that demand strengthened despite weather constraints.

However, EBITDA remained essentially stable, as positive trends in Australia were offset by increased marketing and implementation costs of the Resource Efficiency Transformation Plan.

The company posted negative EPS that widened vs last year. This means that profitability is in a transitional phase.

Pass sales present a mixed picture.

Units dropped 2%, but revenues increased by 3% as clients preferred more expensive options.

After the 20th of September, there was improvement and sales increased by 6% in value. This rise showed that broader social media and influencer engagement is beginning to pay off.

The Company has, at the moment, 2.3 million "committed" visitors for the season to come. Those are expected to cover about 74% of total skier visits and generate almost $1 billion in preseason revenue.

Liquidity is still strong at $1.5 billion while net debt is three times trailing EBITDA. This is a manageable level that still requires steady profitability in the second half.

Capex for 2026 are near $235 million, increased by upgrades to lifts, dining and technology systems.

Overall, the financials indicate a foundation for recovery but the margin of safety is thin. Especially if weather conditions are bad or consumers decrease their spending.

Corporate Developments, Strategy and Technology

The most important strategic development is the shift to a more communicative and digital approach.

Greater emphasis is placed on paid media, social channels and influencer marketing.

The announcements show that the Company recognized that the traditional email engagement is no longer enough to reach younger visitors.

The new model is built around three central pillars.

First, deeper integration of lift tickets with the pass funnel.

Epic Friends tickets are introduced, which offer a 50% discount and act as a basis for future conversion of guests into passholders.

In addition, guests can use up to $ 175 from a lift ticket purchase as a credit for a future pass, reducing the cost of entering the loyalty program.

Second, the company is expanding dynamic pricing policies.

The new 30% discount for those who buy a ticket a month in advance acts as a call-to-action, transferring demand earlier in the season.

The example of tickets at Vail Mountain, where the price is reduced to about $ 229 from $ 335 per day, points out a strategy of balancing price and volume.

Which in turn may boost traffic in an environment where pass sales have reached maturity.

Third, the technological upgrade concerns both functions and the guest experience.

The "My Epic" App is going to support purchases directly through Apple Pay and Google Pay.

The company will also digitize the "Ski School" experience and upgrades its ecommerce platform.

These changes are expected to improve the conversion rate but it will take time to see the results.

Operationally, the company is moving forward with significant investments in lift upgrades at Park City and Whistler Blackcomb and in remote avalanche control technologies.

At the same time, the company is entering a multiyear project that is targeting to improve dining services, responding to trends that intensified after the pandemic.

According to management, these investments together with the Resource Efficiency Transformation Plan will deliver $38 million in savings during 2026.

The picture that is presented is of a company trying to rebuild momentum via diversification of the sources of visitation, improvement of the experience and a more aggressive reliance on data.

The effectiveness of this strategy will be judged over the coming years, especially if weather conditions continue to affect the model.

Valuation

The stock price is at $142 and a P/E of around 21x and trades at a valuation that requires ongoing EBITDA expansion and a stabilization of operating costs.

In order to evaluate the prospects i will present you the following scenarios.

The bull scenario involves a smooth season and success in efforts to increase lift ticket visitation.

In this case, a forward P/E near 23x would not be excessive, as the company would show improved profitability and an increase in dividend payments.

The stock could climb at $155 (+10%). This could give the potential of a slight upward movement.

In the base scenario, we assume stable operating performance but a small lag in pass sales and limited contribution from new marketing strategies.

The P/E could move to current levels of 21x, with a stock price at about the same levels, around $142.

This scenario corresponds to the discount that the company needs more time for the changes to pay off.

The bear case scenario concerns a difficult period with persistent variable weather conditions and weak consumer sentiment.

In this environment, P/E could be at around 17x-18x meaning greater future uncertainty. The price could fall to $115-120 range (-15%).

The valuation shows that the market has already priced in significant recovery. The reality however is different as there is instability derived by external factors.

The stock has upside potential but only if it becomes clearer that the new strategies lead to solid growth.

The stock is performing poorly, as over the last 5 years it has dropped 50%, with 2025 having losses of 25%.

The dividend of $2.22 per quarter is not sufficient to offset the stock’s decline. The seemingly high yield (6.2%) refers to the large drop in the stock and not to any increase in it.

It paid the same dividend at $220 (4%), last year, while financials show that the dividend could be decreased in the coming period.

Risks

The main risk remains weather. Delayed snowfall in the Rocky Mountain region and Tahoe created a weak start to the season and had an immediate impact on booking momentum.

Management notes that the outlook for the rest of the period is exposed to external forces that cannot be managed.

A second risk has to do with the behavior of the consumer.

Vail has invested in more premium products and expects demand to stay strong in higher tier passes.

However, lower budget groups could reduce their visits. This is already happening in some markets in the U.S.A.

The company is trying to lower this risk with new discounts but the balance between price and size is difficult.

Profitability is the third risk. The increase in spending on marketing and technology supports long term growth but it compresses margins in an environment where operating costs are rising.

The Resource Efficiency Transformation Plan provides some compensation, but the full return of the plan remains in the future.

With a P/E of 21x, the market has already priced in a significant part of the future recovery.

The success of the new strategies requires at least two seasons to show results and any deviation from expectations could lead to a correction.

The management is optimistic but cant rule out volatility while the biggest part of the EBITDA is created in a few weeks, increasing the seasonal risk.

Conclusion

Vail Resorts is in a critical turning point.

Management appears determined to change its business model giving more emphasis on improving the guest experience, technological upgrades and dynamic pricing.

The company's finances remain healthy but margin pressures are present and expected to continue for at least part of 2026.

The stock is already valued at levels that reflect a positive future scenario and therefore my recommendation is Hold.

There is room for upside, but the risks are significant. Better visibility is needed before considering a more aggressive position in MTN stock.

data.title: Omega Flex: No Improvement In Sight
data.description:
Introduction

This article follows up on previous coverage of Omega Flex, Inc. (OFLX), a manufacturer of flexible metal hose with automotive and marina fuel applications. It has a presence in both Europe and the USA, and while that is a benefit, the share price has been sliding for a few years now, losing about 75% in the past three years.

A closer look at the Q3 results

In the third quarter of the current financial year, Omega Flex saw a 2.5% decrease in its revenue in combination with a 1% increase in its COGS, and this had a negative impact of approximately 5% on its gross profit, which came in at $14.6 million in Q3 2025 compared to $15.4 million in Q3 2024.

Meanwhile, some other expenses also increased. While the selling expenses remained pretty flat, Omega Flex had to deal with a 6% increase in its G&A expenses, while it also saw a 25% increase in its engineering expenses. These higher expenses had a negative impact on the operating profit, which decreased by roughly a quarter to just under $4.2 million.

And the bad news didn’t end there. As interest rates are decreasing, Omega Flex is now also recording a lower interest income on its cash pile. Taking all these elements into consideration, the total net profit attributable to the common shareholders of Omega Flex was approximately $3.7 million for an EPS of $0.37.

Omega Flex currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.34 per share, which remains fully covered. But with a payout ratio of around 93%, the dividend coverage ratio is coming under pressure.

Looking at the 9M 2025 results, we see a similar evolution. The revenue is slightly under pressure while the COGS doesn’t really decrease, resulting in compressed gross margins. And with slightly higher other operating expenses, the operating profit is under even more pressure.

In my previous article on Omega Flex, I also looked at the company’s cash flow statement to get a better understanding of the underlying cash flows, and in the first nine months of the year, Omega Flex reported an operating cash flow of $9.7 million.

This includes the impact of a $3.1 million investment in working capital elements, which means the adjusted operating cash flow was approximately $12.8 million. After deducting the $1.6 million in capital expenditures, the underlying free cash flow was $11.2 million, representing $1.11/share. That’s in line with the reported net income.

The balance sheet remained healthy with almost $50 million in cash and a positive working capital position of approximately $67 million. The balance sheet does not contain any debt, and with a net cash position representing approximately $4.5/share (or 16% of the current market cap), Omega Flex is still in excellent financial shape.

That being said, a strong balance sheet cannot be the sole reason to consider buying Omega Flex, and the net financial performance of the company in the first nine months of the year doesn’t really excite me.

Investment thesis

While the quarterly dividend of 34 cents per share currently reflects a healthy dividend yield of 5%, the dividends may soon come under pressure as the payout ratio increased to 90% in the first nine months of the year and even 93% in the third quarter. While it is possible for Omega Flex to use its strong cash position to keep the dividend unchanged – even if the payout ratio would (temporarily) exceed 100%—the stock is losing its appeal as an income idea.

I still label the stock as a "Hold" as income investors may still like the generous dividend yield. But I cannot justify a "Buy" rating given the weak performance this year and increased pressure on operating margins.

data.title: Credo: The Vendor AI Giants Can't Replace
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Credo Technology’s (CRDO) bull case is rooted in its shift from component vendor to reliability architect for AI clusters. Its AEC (Active Electrical Cable), offering up to 1,000x better reliability and far lower power anchor a 272% YoY revenue surge and make Credo indispensable as hyperscalers battle link-flap downtime. With ZeroFlap optics, ALCs, deep telemetry integration, and purpose-built SerDes for the 200G era, Credo locks in customers and expands a $10 billion+ TAM. In short, Credo sells uptime and that reliability moat keeps widening, supporting a strong buy rating for the stock.

The Reliability-Centric System Moat

Credo Technology is surpassing revenue figures with quarterly beats. My analysis is based on Credo’s shift from a component supplier to a vital system-level architect for AI clusters. Credo sells uptime in the high-stakes environment of AI training. For these, clusters cost billions and downtime destroys millions. The reality is that system reliability has become the most expensive variable in AI infrastructure. Credo is capitalizing (annual topline growth) on the same by capturing market share. I see that AEC topline grew double digits QoQ in Q2FY26 and led a 272% YoY revenue expansion.

However, the raw % hides the real thing at work. AECs deliver up to 1,000x better reliability than traditional laser-based optical modules and consume around half the power. This statistic is the support of CRDO stock price. When hyperscalers build 100,000 GPU clusters, a Link Flap (intermittent connection failure) delays time-to-revenue. Thus, solving this physics problem with SerDes (Serializer/Deserializer) architecture, Credo locks-in clients as the alternative is operational failure.

I see this moat expanding rapidly with ZeroFlap optics, Active LED Cables (ALCs), and OmniConnect gearboxes. Credo is applying this reliability feature to the entire rack and row like ALCs extend the reach of copper-like reliability up to 30 meters for row-scale networks. This factor doubles the addressable market for the cabling business and Credo intends to own the entire stack for ALCs. By this Credo is mirroring the vertical integration model used for AECs.

The market lead is visible in the customer ramp. Credo now has 4 clients providing over 10% of revenue each (at 42%, 24%, 16%, and 11%). A fifth customer is also ramping. This diversification prevents dependence on a single architecture. If hyperscalers use merchant silicon or custom ASICs, the demand for reliable interconnects remains stable. How? AECs have already become the de facto standard for inter-rack connectivity up to 7 meters.

Further, I see a shift in Credo’s monetization of this reliability. Link Health telemetry data creates autonomous detection of degrading links before they fail, so it is used as a check engine light for the AI data center. This software capability shifts a hardware purchase into an intelligent network asset. By integrating this telemetry into the switch-level SDK, Credo integrates itself deeply into the client's network OS.

This software-hardware configuration makes displacing Credo costly and technically difficult. As a competitor/alternative, they need to replicate the entire telemetry and reliability standards. Moreover, the future impact of the 100G to 200G transition also boosts the upside for topline growth as 100G per lane solutions are one of the fastest-growing segments. As speeds double to 200G, signal integrity becomes harder to maintain, so the value of Credo's purpose-built SerDes increases. The step function improvement in reliability drives AEC expansion in these higher-speed generations and this tech advantage may widen the gap between Credo and the next alternative.

Finally, the manufacturing seals this advantage as Credo has no concern regarding AEC production quantity. The vertical integration pushes Credo to control component availability and insulate itself from industry shortages. This capability in turn gives clients the reason to involve Credo into next-generation clusters. With a TAM now exceeding $10 billion, triple the size from 18 months ago, I conclude that CRDO stock premium valuation is supported by this expanding reliability-focused monopoly (on short-reach and medium-reach AI connectivity).

Valuation And Concentration Risk With Non-Linear Order Patterns

There is a big disconnect between the stock's perfection-priced valuation and the lump nature of its revenue. The topline growth rates are high but the street has put a multiple that leaves zero margin for error. There can be a scenario where the stock price can suffer severe corrections if the business fails to move in the straight line that the valuation demands.

Looking at a TTM Non-GAAP P/E of 95x and a TTM GAAP P/E of 153x with the TTM EV/Sales ratio sits at ~37x. These are big numbers as valuation of 37x sales means that Credo may experience continued hyper-growth for years. However, as the largest client responsible for 42% of topline in Q2 and took a bit of a pause in the current quarter. As per the management ramps at any single hyperscaler are never really linear.

As Credo trades at ~100x earnings, the street consider it as a software proxy with recurring predictable revenue. But Credo is not a SaaS company and it is a hardware supplier selling into capex cycles with top four clients accounting for ~93% of topline. If the client with the 42% share decides to digest inventory for two quarters instead of one, Credo's topline growth can flatten instantly. The stock price is pricing in 100%+ forward growth for topline and may move away from these expectations and can face hard corrections. The dependence on a handful of decision-makers makes Credo’s topline highly vulnerable to shifts in architecture and deployment schedules.

However, based on consensus forecasts, CRDO's forward P/S compresses meaningfully from ~24x in FY26 to roughly 17x in FY27 as revenue steps up to an estimated $1.63 billion. That normalization makes the valuation far more reasonable relative to its growth curve, positioning FY27 as the first year the stock screens genuinely attractive on a sales-multiple basis.

Further, the OpEx is resetting to a new higher base. Q3 OpEx may hit $70 million with a 22% increase at the midpoint. Management links this to hiring the Hyperlume team and other project spending. The increase is necessary for long-term product development, but this immediate hike in fixed costs raises the breakeven point up. If revenue hits an air pocket based on lumpy hyperscale orders, the operating leverage can reverse and may push the multiple compression.

For clients, all want multiple suppliers as per the management. Credo currently has a near-monopoly in its niche. The high margins (67.7% gross margin) and massive TAM ($10 Billion+) act as blood in the water as Credo filed ITC complaints to protect its IP. This is indicating that copycats are already appearing and if a second good enough source comes up and if cheaper, the hyperscalers (ruthless about cost) will diversify.

Also, the fluid tariff regime adds another layer of unpredictability as I see. Supply chains tightly wound and manufacturing spread across Asia, any geopolitical friction may disrupt the flow of these high-value players. The market ignores this risk at current price levels, but I think even a minor delay in shipments due to customs/tariffs may lead to a quarterly miss. Analyst price targets have moved in lockstep with the stock rise, creating a loop of expectation.

Analysts in the Q2-call asking about 200G and 1.6T speeds before 100G is even fully saturated. So, this relentless push for the next big thing blinds the street about the operational risks. The expectation for net income quadruples YoY. If Credo delivers only a tripling of income due to a single client pushing a deployment out by 90 days, the algorithmic stock selling will hurt CRDO stock price.

Takeaway

Credo sells the one thing AI data centers can’t afford to lose, uptime. Its AEC-driven reliability, expanding telemetry stack, and dominance in next-gen 200G SerDes make it a core systems partner, not a parts supplier. As reliability becomes the new currency of AI, Credo’s moat, and its runway, continues to strengthen.

data.title: NXP Semiconductors: Cycle Noise Fading, Earnings Story Takes The Wheel In FY26
data.description:
Investment Thesis

NXPI growth algorithm is now based significantly more upon the amount of additional silicon content added to each system than on the number of units sold. The trend toward software-defined vehicles, increased use of advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS), increased penetration of electrically driven powertrains, and enhanced secure in-vehicle networks continue to increase silicon content independently of production increases. The content additions were made throughout the downcycle but were obscured by the customers' inventory digestion. As the distribution channels normalize, that embedded design momentum will be reflected in the reported revenue. Additionally, industrial and IoT represent a second structural leg of growth, which is primarily influenced by factory automation, energy management, and edge computing. These markets are generally steadier and less impacted by the auto cycle than other segments of the market.

While the recent run-up in NXPI is certainly impressive, in my view, NXPI is still not being traded as a structurally improving compounding platform. The stock is still reflective of skepticism regarding the duration of the cycle and not confidence in the normalized earnings power of the company. When the revenues and margins of the company return to normalized levels in FY26-FY27, the upside in NXPI will be much less related to a snap-back in volumes and more related to the market's gradual recognition of the improved quality of the company's earnings profile. From a medium-term perspective, I believe the risk-reward skew of NXPI is very attractive, and I am positive on the company.

Company Overview

NXP Semiconductors is a global leader in mixed-signal and embedded processing solutions that focus on automotive, industrial, and secure connectivity applications. The company is headquartered in Eindhoven, the Netherlands, and has ~33k employees globally (including ~11.6k R&D employees) with operations across manufacturing facilities, research and development centers, and sales offices in more than 30 countries. The company's largest end market is automotive, with exposure to all areas of ADAS (advanced driver assistance systems), radar, battery management, in-vehicle networking, and vehicle security. The company's second largest area of focus is industrial & IoT, where the company serves factory automation, energy management, and intelligent edge systems. In addition to these areas, the company is a global leader in secure identification, NFC (near field communication), and embedded security that is used across mobile, payments, and access control. The company employs a hybrid manufacturing model that combines internal fabrication facilities with third-party foundries to balance control of the supply chain with flexibility in terms of capital expenditures. Because of the long lifecycle nature of their products, the fact that they are used in safety-critical and regulated environments, the company has created high switching costs and durable customer relationships.

Revenue Analysis and Outlook

NXP generated Q3 FY25 revenue of ~$3.17bn, decreased ~2% y/y, but increased ~8% sequentially, indicating that the inventory corrections are behind us and we're seeing normalized ordering behavior. Automotive revenue was strong in Q3 FY25 with $1.84bn in revenue, unchanged from last year, but 6% q/q, reinforcing that automotive content is sustainable even when there isn't unit growth. Industrial & IoT revenue was also positive in Q3 FY25 with revenue of $579mn, a +3% y/y and 6% q/q, showing that the industrial channel is no longer a drag on revenue as the demand for factory automation, energy, and edge computing is stabilizing. Mobile was a bright spot in Q3 FY25 and reported revenue of $430mn with +30% q/q and 6% y/y growth driven by seasonal strength in secure connectivity and UWB. Communications Infrastructure & Other was the weakest end-market in the portfolio in Q3 FY2,5, and revenue was $327mn, a 27% y/y decrease and only a 2% sequential increase.

There is a fundamental difference between how NXP’s FY25 revenues will end the year versus when NXP started FY25. Throughout the first half of FY25, channel digestion of excess inventory across the industrial and communications infrastructure channels mechanically limited the company's reported growth, even as automotive remained a strong operationally. NXP is no longer in the same inventory-digestion mode it was in at the beginning of the year. Going forward, what matters most isn’t a normalization in volumes, although that will occur eventually, but instead a material increase in the proportion of revenue derived from higher-content, structurally growing applications in automotive and industrial.

Going into FY26, the growth engine for NXP will not be based on the number of units sold but rather the increasing content per vehicle sold. Automotive content will increase dollar content per vehicle regardless of the rate of global automobile unit growth. Electrification, advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS) penetration, and the transition to a software-defined vehicle represent multi-year architectural changes rather than cyclical trends. Therefore, even in a low single-digit growth environment in terms of global automobile units, NXP can grow automotive revenue faster than the rest of the industry simply because the amount of semiconductor-intensive content per vehicle continues to increase.

Industrial represents the second major structural pillar in NXP’s business and is emerging once again as a source of growth. Applications such as factory automation, building automation, energy infrastructure, edge computing, and secure industrial connectivity are all applications with high content requirements that align well with NXP’s analog, microcontroller unit (MCU), power management IC (PMIC), and security portfolios. While the quality of growth in the industrial channel is similar to previous cycles, a greater proportion of industrial demand is now being generated by energy efficiency initiatives, grid digitalization, and the rollout of automated manufacturing processes as opposed to solely discretionary capital expenditures. Thus, as the industrial channel begins to normalize, revenue growth from this channel should increasingly become more predictable and less cyclical in nature.

Mobile will likely remain a supporting contributor to NXP’s revenue growth rather than a major growth driver. Mobile provides NXP with elastic volume through secure connectivity, ultra-wideband (UWB) technology, and seasonal ramp-ups in device sales, but it will not dictate NXP’s long-term growth trajectory. Conversely, communications infrastructure and other continue to contract structurally as NXP intentionally reduces its exposure to lower-return radio frequency (RF) and legacy networking products. However, the significance of this contraction is decreasing at the NXP level as communications infrastructure, and others continue to diminish in terms of revenue weight.

Therefore, NXP is developing a cleaner portfolio with respect to communications infrastructure and others as the years pass, even as the near-term optical impact may be a drag on growth. When viewed collectively, NXP exited FY25 as a transition year at the company level in which headline growth remains in the low single-digit range, not due to weak underlying demand, but as a result of the lingering effect of the normalization of the industrial channel and the intentional contraction of communications infrastructure. Rather than the growth rate of FY25, the economic significance of the year lies in the fact that NXP exited the year with automotive and industrial representing a significantly larger portion of the revenue base than previously.

The real growth inflection point for NXP occurs in FY26 and beyond. As the industrial recovery becomes more fully reflected in shipments and automotive content continues to compound, NXP should be positioned to deliver high-single-digit consolidated revenue growth. Importantly, this growth is not expected to be driven by unit-volume cyclicality, but by the continued addition of safety, compute, connectivity, power, and security content across both vehicle and industrial end-markets. By that stage, communications infrastructure would have diminished to a marginal offset rather than a structural headwind, while mobile would continue to provide elastic volume without becoming a core growth dependency. At the same time, automotive and industrial would function as a stable and compounding revenue engine for the company.

Margin Analysis and Outlook

Q3 FY25 represented a stabilization in NXP’s gross margin (GAAP) levels at approximately mid-56%, which reflected a stabilization in the levels of utilization and a more favorable revenue mix. Operating margin also demonstrated stability in the low-30s (28.1%) in Q3 FY25, notwithstanding that industrial volumes were still below normalized levels, and communications infrastructure and other (CI&O) continue to negatively affect the overall blended margin of the company. From a key perspective, margins stopped declining in Q3 FY25, even though revenue was not yet at peak cycle levels, which indicates that the cost structure has largely adjusted to the new lower revenue base.

Structurally, NXP’s margin profile today is less influenced by short cycles in communications compared to prior cycles. Automotive and industrial, combined, now comprise a majority of the revenue base and historically have carried structurally higher and more stable margins than those associated with mobile RF or legacy networking products. Additionally, as CI&O continues to decrease in relation to the total revenue mix, the natural reduction in blended margin volatility is already evident. The same was observed in Q3 FY25, in which the sequential improvement in margins occurred without the need for price increases or consumer-driven volume rebounds.

I expect that in FY26, NXP’s margin expansion will not be based on price increases. Price levels are generally normalized in both automotive and industrial sectors, and competitive intensity in mobile remains very high. Therefore, the next stage of margin expansion will be driven by operating leverage, specifically as industrial volumes continue to recover and automotive content grows. A mid-single-digit revenue growth environment will provide NXP with sufficient opportunity to realize meaningful operating leverage, as NXP has already absorbed the majority of its cost reset during FY24-FY25.

Accordingly, I anticipate that gross margins will gradually rebuild to the mid-56.5% to ~57.0% range over the course of FY26, and that operating margins will build towards the mid-30s, absent further macro disruptions. Such an outcome would represent a normalization period, during which utilization, mix, and incremental volume work together once again.

By FY27, NXP’s margins should transition from normalization to structural expansion. As automotive and industrial continue to grow in their shares of the revenue base and as software-attached compute, secure connectivity, and higher value system content are scaled, the overall blended margin of the company should be structurally above the current margin range. At that point, margin expansion will no longer be a function of cost control; rather, it will be an indicator of the quality and sustainability of the next peak in the margin profile. The margin engine will be increasingly tied to long cycles in automotive and industrial platforms and less so to short cycles in communications or opportunistic pricing.
